sectionName: Chemical & Physical Foundations of Biological Systems

questions.passage: A biochemist prepares solutions for experiments. She dissolves 58.4 g of NaCl (MW = 58.4 g/mol) in water to make 1.0 L of solution, producing a 1.0 M NaCl solution. She studies how dissolved solutes affect boiling point, freezing point, osmotic pressure, and vapor pressure. She notes that NaCl dissociates into NaвҒә and ClвҒ», giving a van't Hoff factor (i) of approximately 2. She also examines solubility rules, the common ion effect, and Ksp equilibria for sparingly soluble salts.
questions.stem: The 1.0 M NaCl solution has an effective particle concentration (osmolarity) of approximately:

choices.label: A
choices.text: 2.0 Osmol/L вҖ” NaCl dissociates into NaвҒә and ClвҒ» (i вүҲ 2), so osmolarity = i Г— molarity = 2 Г— 1.0 = 2.0 Osmol/L

choices.label: B
choices.text: 1.0 Osmol/L (same as molarity because dissociation does not matter)

choices.label: C
choices.text: 0.5 Osmol/L

choices.label: D
choices.text: 3.0 Osmol/L

explanations.A: Correct. Osmolarity accounts for the total concentration of dissolved particles. NaCl is a strong electrolyte that fully dissociates: NaCl вҶ’ NaвҒә + ClвҒ», producing 2 particles per formula unit (i вүҲ 2). Osmolarity = i Г— M = 2 Г— 1.0 = 2.0 Osmol/L. This is why NaCl has a greater effect on colligative properties (boiling point elevation, freezing point depression, osmotic pressure) than a non-electrolyte at the same molar concentration. Glucose (a non-electrolyte, i = 1) at 1.0 M gives only 1.0 Osmol/L.
explanations.B: This ignores dissociation. For non-electrolytes (glucose, urea), osmolarity equals molarity. But for electrolytes like NaCl, the number of dissolved particles exceeds the number of formula units dissolved.
explanations.C: 0.5 Osmol/L is less than the molarity, which is impossible for an electrolyte that dissociates вҖ” the number of particles must be at least equal to the number of formula units.
explanations.D: 3.0 Osmol/L would be the osmolarity of a 1:2 electrolyte like CaClвӮӮ (CaВІвҒә + 2ClвҒ», i вүҲ 3) at 1.0 M, not NaCl.

questions.stem: Boiling point elevation (О”Tb) for this NaCl solution is calculated as:

choices.label: A
choices.text: О”Tb = iKbm, where i is the van't Hoff factor, Kb is the ebullioscopic constant, and m is molality вҖ” for NaCl (i вүҲ 2), the elevation is approximately twice that of a non-electrolyte at the same concentration

choices.label: B
choices.text: О”Tb = Kbm only, because the van't Hoff factor is always 1

choices.label: C
choices.text: О”Tb = m/Kb

choices.label: D
choices.text: boiling point is lowered, not elevated, by adding solute

explanations.A: Correct. Colligative properties depend on the number of dissolved particles, not their identity. О”Tb = iKbm. For 1.0 m NaCl with i = 2 and Kb = 0.512 В°C/m (water): О”Tb = 2 Г— 0.512 Г— 1.0 = 1.024 В°C. New boiling point вүҲ 101.0 В°C. The van't Hoff factor is critical for electrolytes. For glucose (i = 1): О”Tb = 0.512 В°C вҖ” half the NaCl elevation at the same concentration.
explanations.B: i = 1 applies only to non-electrolytes (glucose, sucrose, urea). For strong electrolytes, i > 1 and must be included. Ignoring i underestimates the colligative effect.
explanations.C: Dividing m by Kb is dimensionally incorrect and inverts the relationship. О”Tb is directly proportional to both Kb and m.
explanations.D: Adding a nonvolatile solute always elevates the boiling point (more energy needed to achieve a vapor pressure equal to atmospheric pressure). Boiling point depression does not occur. Freezing point is depressed, which is the opposite colligative effect.

questions.stem: Osmotic pressure (ПҖ) drives water across a semipermeable membrane from:

choices.label: A
choices.text: the side with higher solute concentration to the side with lower solute concentration

choices.label: B
choices.text: the side with lower solute concentration (higher water concentration) to the side with higher solute concentration вҖ” ПҖ = iMRT

choices.label: C
choices.text: both sides equally, so no net water movement occurs

choices.label: D
choices.text: the membrane itself generates the pressure independent of solute concentration

explanations.B: Correct. Osmosis: water moves from the dilute side (high water activity) to the concentrated side (low water activity) across a semipermeable membrane. Osmotic pressure ПҖ = iMRT (van't Hoff equation), where i = van't Hoff factor, M = molarity, R = gas constant, T = temperature (K). For 1.0 M NaCl at 310 K: ПҖ = 2 Г— 1.0 Г— 0.0821 Г— 310 вүҲ 50.9 atm. This enormous pressure is why red blood cells lyse in hypotonic solutions and crenate in hypertonic solutions.
explanations.A: Water moves toward higher solute concentration, not away from it. Solute cannot cross the semipermeable membrane, so water moves to equalize concentrations.
explanations.C: Net water movement occurs whenever there is a concentration difference across the membrane. Equal movement (no net flow) occurs only when concentrations are equal on both sides (isotonic conditions).
explanations.D: Osmotic pressure arises from the concentration difference across the membrane, not from the membrane itself. The membrane simply provides the selective barrier that allows osmotic pressure to manifest.

questions.stem: The Ksp of AgCl is 1.8 Г— 10вҒ»В№вҒ°. The molar solubility of AgCl in pure water is:

choices.label: A
choices.text: 1.8 Г— 10вҒ»В№вҒ° M

choices.label: B
choices.text: 1.8 Г— 10вҒ»вҒө M

choices.label: C
choices.text: 0.9 Г— 10вҒ»В№вҒ° M

choices.label: D
choices.text: 1.34 Г— 10вҒ»вҒө M вҖ” from Ksp = [AgвҒә][ClвҒ»] = sВІ, so s = вҲҡ(1.8 Г— 10вҒ»В№вҒ°) = 1.34 Г— 10вҒ»вҒө M

explanations.D: Correct. AgCl вҮҢ AgвҒә + ClвҒ». If s mol/L dissolves, then [AgвҒә] = s and [ClвҒ»] = s. Ksp = [AgвҒә][ClвҒ»] = s Г— s = sВІ. s = вҲҡKsp = вҲҡ(1.8 Г— 10вҒ»В№вҒ°) = вҲҡ(18 Г— 10вҒ»В№В№) вүҲ 4.24 Г— 10вҒ»вҒөВ·вҒө вүҲ 1.34 Г— 10вҒ»вҒө M. This means only 1.34 Г— 10вҒ»вҒө mol of AgCl dissolves per liter of water. AgCl is essentially insoluble, consistent with the solubility rules (most chlorides are soluble except AgвҒә, PbВІвҒә, HgвӮӮВІвҒә).
explanations.A: 1.8 Г— 10вҒ»В№вҒ° M is the Ksp value itself, not the molar solubility. For a 1:1 salt like AgCl, solubility is the square root of Ksp.
explanations.B: 1.8 Г— 10вҒ»вҒө M results from taking the wrong root or power. The correct calculation is s = вҲҡ(1.8 Г— 10вҒ»В№вҒ°) вүҲ 1.34 Г— 10вҒ»вҒө.
explanations.C: 0.9 Г— 10вҒ»В№вҒ° is simply half the Ksp value, which is not the correct operation. Molar solubility requires taking the square root of Ksp for a 1:1 salt.

questions.stem: The common ion effect predicts that adding NaCl to a saturated AgCl solution would:

choices.label: A
choices.text: increase AgCl solubility because more ions are available

choices.label: B
choices.text: have no effect on AgCl solubility

choices.label: C
choices.text: decrease AgCl solubility вҖ” the added ClвҒ» shifts the dissolution equilibrium to the left (Le Chatelier's principle), causing AgCl to precipitate

choices.label: D
choices.text: double the Ksp of AgCl

explanations.C: Correct. Adding NaCl introduces common ClвҒ» ions. By Le Chatelier's principle, the increased [ClвҒ»] shifts the AgCl dissolution equilibrium to the left: AgCl вҮҢ AgвҒә + ClвҒ». The ion product Q = [AgвҒә][ClвҒ»] temporarily exceeds Ksp, causing AgCl to precipitate until equilibrium is restored at a lower [AgвҒә]. The solubility of AgCl decreases. Quantitatively, if [ClвҒ»] from NaCl = 0.1 M, then [AgвҒә] = Ksp/[ClвҒ»] = 1.8 Г— 10вҒ»В№вҒ°/0.1 = 1.8 Г— 10вҒ»вҒ№ M вҖ” far less than the 1.34 Г— 10вҒ»вҒө M in pure water.
explanations.A: The common ion effect decreases solubility, not increases it. Le Chatelier's principle drives the equilibrium toward the solid (less dissolved) when product ion concentration increases.
explanations.B: Adding a common ion significantly affects the equilibrium. The dissolution equilibrium shifts to accommodate the new [ClвҒ»], reducing [AgвҒә] to maintain Ksp.
explanations.D: Ksp is a thermodynamic constant at a given temperature вҖ” it does not change with the addition of common ions. The ion product adjusts (precipitation occurs), but Ksp itself remains 1.8 Г— 10вҒ»В№вҒ°.

questions.stem: Raoult's law states that the vapor pressure of a solvent above a solution is:

choices.label: A
choices.text: higher than the pure solvent's vapor pressure

choices.label: B
choices.text: equal to the solute's vapor pressure

choices.label: C
choices.text: independent of the solute concentration

choices.label: D
choices.text: P = X_solvent Г— PВ°_solvent вҖ” the vapor pressure is reduced in proportion to the mole fraction of the nonvolatile solute

explanations.B: For nonvolatile solutes (like NaCl or glucose), the solute has essentially zero vapor pressure. The solution's vapor pressure comes entirely from the solvent, reduced by the presence of solute.
explanations.A: Adding a nonvolatile solute always lowers the vapor pressure, not raises it. Solute molecules at the surface reduce the number of solvent molecules that can escape into the vapor phase.
explanations.C: Vapor pressure is directly dependent on solute concentration through the mole fraction. Higher solute concentration means lower X_solvent and therefore lower vapor pressure.
explanations.D: Correct. Raoult's law: P_solution = X_solvent Г— PВ°_solvent, where X_solvent is the mole fraction of the solvent and PВ° is the pure solvent vapor pressure. Since X_solvent < 1 when solute is present, P_solution < PВ°. Vapor pressure lowering: О”P = X_solute Г— PВ°_solvent. Nonvolatile solute molecules occupy surface sites, reducing the rate of solvent evaporation. This is the molecular basis for boiling point elevation вҖ” higher temperature is needed to reach atmospheric pressure. Raoult's law applies to ideal dilute solutions.

questions.stem: An isotonic saline solution (0.9% NaCl, ~0.154 M) is used for IV infusion because:

choices.label: A
choices.text: it is hypertonic to blood, causing red blood cells to shrink beneficially

choices.label: B
choices.text: it has the same osmolarity as blood (~300 mOsm/L), so red blood cells neither swell nor shrink вҖ” maintaining cell volume and function

choices.label: C
choices.text: it is hypotonic to blood, causing cells to swell and improve oxygen delivery

choices.label: D
choices.text: NaCl is the only solute that does not interact with cell membranes

explanations.B: Correct. Isotonic solutions have the same effective osmolarity as the intracellular fluid (~300 mOsm/L). Normal saline (0.154 M NaCl, i вүҲ 2) has osmolarity вүҲ 308 mOsm/L, closely matching blood. No net water movement occurs across the RBC membrane, so cells maintain their normal biconcave shape. Hypertonic solutions (>300 mOsm/L) cause crenation (water leaves cells). Hypotonic solutions (<300 mOsm/L) cause swelling and potential lysis (hemolysis).
explanations.A: Hypertonic solutions cause crenation (cell shrinkage), which impairs RBC function. Crenated cells have reduced surface area and cannot efficiently carry oxygen. This is not beneficial.
explanations.C: Hypotonic solutions cause cell swelling and potential lysis (hemolysis), destroying RBCs. This would be harmful, not helpful.
explanations.D: Many solutes are used in IV fluids (dextrose, lactated Ringer's). NaCl is chosen because it provides physiological NaвҒә and ClвҒ» at an isotonic concentration, not because of unique membrane properties.

questions.stem: Freezing point depression (О”Tf) for this NaCl solution is:

choices.label: A
choices.text: calculated from О”Tf = iKfm вҖ” for 1.0 m NaCl (i вүҲ 2, Kf = 1.86 В°C/m for water): О”Tf = 2 Г— 1.86 Г— 1.0 = 3.72 В°C, so the freezing point is approximately -3.72 В°C

choices.label: B
choices.text: 0 В°C, because NaCl does not affect freezing point

choices.label: C
choices.text: +3.72 В°C (the freezing point increases)

choices.label: D
choices.text: calculated without the van't Hoff factor: О”Tf = 1.86 В°C

explanations.A: Correct. О”Tf = iKfm = 2 Г— 1.86 Г— 1.0 = 3.72 В°C. The freezing point is depressed to 0 - 3.72 = -3.72 В°C. This is why salt is used to de-ice roads вҖ” it depresses the freezing point of water, preventing ice formation above -3.72 В°C (for this concentration). CaClвӮӮ (i вүҲ 3) is even more effective because it produces more particles per formula unit.
explanations.B: All dissolved solutes depress the freezing point of a solvent. This is a colligative property вҖ” it depends on particle concentration, not solute identity.
explanations.C: Solutes always lower (depress) the freezing point, never raise it. The solute disrupts the ordered crystal lattice of ice, requiring lower temperatures for solidification.
explanations.D: This calculation ignores the van't Hoff factor (i = 2 for NaCl). For electrolytes, omitting i underestimates the colligative effect by a factor equal to i.

questions.stem: Molality (m) differs from molarity (M) in that molality is:

choices.label: A
choices.text: grams of solute per liter of solution

choices.label: B
choices.text: moles of solute per liter of solution, which changes with temperature

choices.label: C
choices.text: moles of solute per kilogram of solvent вҖ” it is temperature-independent because mass does not change with temperature, unlike volume

choices.label: D
choices.text: identical to molarity in all cases

explanations.A: Grams per liter is a mass concentration (g/L), not molality. Molality uses moles (not grams) and kilograms of solvent (not liters of solution).
explanations.B: Moles per liter of solution is the definition of molarity, not molality. Molarity is temperature-dependent because solution volume changes with temperature.
explanations.C: Correct. Molality (m) = moles of solute / kg of solvent. Molarity (M) = moles of solute / L of solution. Molality is temperature-independent because mass does not change with temperature, while volume (and thus molarity) varies with thermal expansion/contraction. Colligative property equations (О”Tb, О”Tf) use molality for this reason. For dilute aqueous solutions, molality вүҲ molarity because the density of water is ~1 kg/L.
explanations.D: Molality and molarity have different definitions and units. They are approximately equal only for dilute aqueous solutions (where 1 L of solution вүҲ 1 kg of solvent).

questions.stem: A solution containing 0.1 M glucose (non-electrolyte) has a lower osmotic pressure than 0.1 M NaCl because:

choices.label: A
choices.text: glucose has a lower molecular weight than NaCl

choices.label: B
choices.text: glucose does not dissolve in water as well as NaCl

choices.label: C
choices.text: glucose does not dissociate into ions, so its effective particle concentration (0.1 Osmol/L) is half that of NaCl (0.2 Osmol/L) вҖ” osmotic pressure depends on total particle count, not molecular identity

choices.label: D
choices.text: glucose molecules are larger and exert less pressure

explanations.C: Correct. Osmotic pressure is a colligative property вҖ” it depends only on the total number of dissolved particles. Glucose (CвӮҶHвӮҒвӮӮOвӮҶ, non-electrolyte, i = 1) at 0.1 M gives 0.1 Osmol/L. NaCl (strong electrolyte, i вүҲ 2) at 0.1 M gives ~0.2 Osmol/L. By ПҖ = iMRT, the NaCl solution has approximately twice the osmotic pressure. This is why 5% dextrose (glucose) and 0.9% NaCl can both be isotonic despite different molar concentrations вҖ” the dissociation of NaCl doubles its effective particle count.
explanations.A: Molecular weight does not directly determine osmotic pressure. What matters is the number of particles per liter (osmolarity), not the mass of each particle.
explanations.B: Both glucose and NaCl are highly soluble in water. Solubility is not the issue вҖ” the difference in osmotic pressure comes from NaCl's dissociation into two ions.
explanations.D: Particle size does not determine osmotic pressure for ideal dilute solutions. Colligative properties are independent of solute identity вҖ” only particle count matters.
